rescue_reasoning: Policy statement targets walking infrastructure: "Improve sidewalks and crossings on arterial streets" to improve connectivity. This reasonably supports a mitigation mechanism via active transport and reduced car dependence (consistent with canonical mechanism: active_transport_infrastructure_improvement → modal_shift_to_walking_and_cycling). While no explicit emissions/climate token appears in the source (gate_failure_reason), climate_screen is "explicit_self", which directs keeping the row. Primary category "Mitigation" matches the modal-shift pathway.
policy_statement: Improve sidewalks and crossings on arterial streets to connect Urban Centers and Villages.
mechanism_description: Improving sidewalks and crossings makes walking safer and more convenient, which can increase walking trips and reduce vehicle travel and associated transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport infrastructure improvement → modal shift (car → walk/bike) → ↓ VMT & fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Cities build/upgrade pedestrian and bicycle infrastructure (e.g., sidewalks, protected crossings, bikeways, intersection safety) that changes travel behavior and trip choice. (2) Process: Improved safety, connectivity, and convenience increases the generalized attractiveness of walking/cycling and reduces reliance on private vehicles, lowering VMT and associated gasoline/diesel combustion (and often congestion-related inefficiency). (3) Outcome: Lower tailpipe CO₂ (and some co-emitted climate forcers) from reduced vehicle travel, yielding net transportation-sector emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced on-road fuel combustion from vehicle-miles traveled (VMT) avoided due to modal shift to walking/cycling.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: active-transport infrastructure induces modal shift away from car trips, reducing fuel combustion and GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: while there can be co-benefits (safety, health, equity, local air quality) and some resource-efficiency elements, the primary climate effect across this mechanism is direct emissions reduction from avoided motorized travel.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a project’s main purpose were heat-risk reduction (shade trees) or flood management, it could tilt toward adaptation/NBS; however, this canonical mechanism is explicitly about walking/cycling uptake reducing vehicle travel. Electrification context does not change the dominant pathway here; it still mitigates by reducing energy use and remaining emissions.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy that improves walking/cycling infrastructure to drive modal shift shares the same primary mitigation pathway via reduced VMT and combustion emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: whether the best evidence supports outcomes via safety, network completeness, pricing complements, or land-use interactions should be assessed at the individual policy level, not at this mechanism classification stage.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified only when the “active transport infrastructure” is primarily implemented for non-emissions goals (e.g., stormwater/heat adaptation corridors) with minimal expected modal shift; otherwise mitigation via reduced VMT is dominant.
key_indicators: "Improve sidewalks and crossings"; "arterial streets"; "connect Urban Centers and Villages"; "Actions to Implement"
co_benefits: Improved pedestrian safety, accessibility/mobility, public health (more walking), reduced local air pollution and noise.
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit funding mechanism or design standard is specified, but the wording implies a public works/capital improvement program rather than a regulatory or informational instrument.
